decisions.why: Same SHA-256 gate as existing dashboards. Read-only view. Prevents casual discovery.

decisions.what: meridian-demos as GitHub Pages deploy target
decisions.why: Pages enabled, public repo. Private repo (meridian) can't use Pages on free plan.

decisions.what: Pre-bake model replaces browser fetch for mesh data
decisions.why: GitHub Pages can't reach Tailscale IPs. Mesh data baked at generation time via SSH/cron.
Denver agreed to pay. Invoice sent via PayPal to Merchant Business Systems.

decisions.what: API-first model — plug into Go High Level, don't rip and replace
decisions.why: Build trust first. Migrate platforms later. Denver already on GHL at $300/mo.

decisions.what: Data sovereignty — brain on MERIDIAN infra, not client hardware
decisions.why: Client keeps data/results but not the engine. Prevents IP leak.

decisions.what: Client data in main repo (not per-client repos) until 5+ clients
decisions.why: Per-client repos add operational overhead. Directory in monorepo sufficient at 3 clients.

decisions.what: self-hosted runner for GitHub Actions (not ubuntu-latest)
decisions.why: Private repo on free plan can't use GitHub-hosted runners. Self-hosted runners currently offline.

decisions.what: Progressive enhancement — Gateway API live, dashboard-data.json static fallback
decisions.why: Dashboard works everywhere: Tailscale (live), GitHub Pages (static), customer VPS (their Gateway).

decisions.what: React 18 CDN for Command Center (same as meshdash.html)
decisions.why: Single file, no build step, component reuse from 4 existing React pages.

decisions.what: Option C (cron push from prime) for mesh metrics
decisions.why: Simplest. No new services. Decoupled freshness. Existing autopull pattern.

decisions.what: Ely's PC added to Tailscale mesh
decisions.why: 32GB RAM, i7, 11TB storage. GPU compute + backup sync node.

decisions.what: Denver maps to Tier 2 (Intelligence OS) — cloud-hosted exoskeleton
decisions.why: Dedicated containerized instance, private document ingestion, custom persona. Upgrade to T3 if sovereignty demands escalate.

calls.title: Audit1
calls.summary: Legal audit of MBM IP Ownership Agreement V3 identifies critical gaps in Exhibits A-C and overly broad provisions (power of attorney, right of first refusal) requiring negotiation before signing.
commitments: Alex: Review agreement and discuss with Dr. Omar before signing
discoveries: Exhibit A (Pre-Existing IP inventory) omits MERIDIAN entirely — MIG, orchestrator, brain, mesh, AgentVox all unprotected if claimed as Company IP
Section 2.5 irrevocable power of attorney allows MBM to sign IP assignments on Alex's behalf unilaterally even after termination
Section 4.3 Right of First Refusal on 'healthcare administration software domain' is strategically veto-like and captures too much of AgentVox addressable market
Exhibit B (BAA) and Exhibit C (compensation rates) remain blank — compliance and rate gaps block execution
Section 1.1(c) 'resources' undefined — could capture personal projects on MBM-provided equipment as Company IP

action_items.what: Populate Exhibit A with comprehensive IP inventory including MERIDIAN, MIG, AgentVox, orchestrator, brain, mesh, voice pipeline
Review and align on Section 2.5 (make power of attorney revocable on termination, extend response window to 20-30 business days)
Negotiate Section 4.3 to narrow ROFR scope to Project-specific deliverables, not entire healthcare domain
Define 'resources' in Section 1.1(c) to exclude general-purpose equipment and cloud services
Complete Exhibit C with locked-in hourly compensation rates
Prepare Business Associate Agreement (Exhibit B) before Project 2 clinical platform work begins to address PHI/HIPAA compliance
Send refined message to Dr. Omar flagging open exhibits and requesting alignment before execution

calls.preview: Legal audit of MBM IP Ownership Agreement V3 identifies critical gaps in Exhibits A-C and overly broad provisions (power of attorney, right of first refusal) requiring negotiation before signing.

calls.title: Ip Agreement V3 Proposed Revisions
calls.summary: Proposed revisions to MBM contractor IP agreement closing ambiguity gaps through detailed definitions, dispute processes, layered architecture codification, and security standards while preserving MBM's absolute company IP ownership.
decisions: Define 'MBM Resources' to exclude general-purpose equipment and infrastructure from IP capture scope
Implement IP classification dispute fast-track with independent attorney review instead of arbitration (§2.7)
Codify three-layer architecture model (Infrastructure/Application/Data) as governing framework for IP classification
Narrow right of first refusal to Project-specific competition only, explicitly excluding Partnership's multi-agent PaaS platform
Add Phase 2 HIPAA gate requiring executed BAA before any PHI-related work or Project 2 commencement (§5.5)
Revise attorney-in-fact powers to exclude disputed IP and terminate automatically on agreement end (§2.5)
Add MBM security standards representation and warranty addressing infrastructure and incident disclosure (§7.6)
Change Exhibit E transfer pricing framework from unilateral imposition to mutual activation with 30-day review period
commitments: Contractor to execute IP assignment documents within 30 business days of MBM request (§2.5)
Parties to attempt IP classification dispute resolution through good faith discussion within 15 business days (§2.7)
Independent IP attorney to provide classification recommendation within 30 days of engagement (§2.7)
MBM to exercise or decline right of first refusal within 30 business days of Contractor notice (§4.3)
MBM to approve/deny subcontractors within 10 business days with deemed approval if no response (§6.4)
MBM to disclose all active or recently resolved security incidents prior to Contractor tool deployment (§7.6)
Either party to provide 60 days written notice before non-renewal (§9.1)
Activating party to provide minimum 30 days review period for Exhibit E transfer pricing framework before implementation
discoveries: Exhibit A was completely unpopulated—single largest risk gap in agreement, enabling IP ownership disputes
Verbal March 10 IP framework agreement was never codified, creating conflicting interpretations of what constitutes Company IP
PHI/HIPAA work had no explicit compliance gate, potentially enabling non-compliant clinical work before BAA execution
Right of first refusal was broad enough to block the Partnership's entire healthcare market addressable market
MBM infrastructure compromise (active GCP compromise mentioned) exposed contractor tool/credential security risks that agreement previously didn't address

action_items.what: Complete and populate Exhibit A with 8 pre-existing IP items, including MERIDIAN Daemon Mesh, MIG, Voice Intelligence Pipeline, and deployment tooling—signed and retained by both before agreement execution
Execute Exhibit B (Business Associate Agreement) before any Project 2 or PHI-related work can commence under Phase 2 gate (§5.5)
Implement IP classification dispute process per §2.7 including good faith discussion triggers, independent attorney engagement protocol, and binding recommendation procedures
Establish and communicate security incident disclosure procedures per §7.6 covering infrastructure access, incident notification timelines, and credential management before Contractor system access
Identify and maintain current inventory of all pre-existing IP and notify MBM of any competitive developments triggering right of first refusal evaluation per §4.3

calls.preview: Proposed revisions to MBM contractor IP agreement closing ambiguity gaps through detailed definitions, dispute processes, layered architecture codification, and security standards while preserving MBM's absolute company IP ownership.

calls.title: Ip Agreement V3 Full Coherence Audit
calls.summary: Full coherence audit of MBM IP Agreement v3 identified critical gaps (missing BAA, compensation rates, SOW), structural contradictions (work-for-hire doctrine inapplicable to independent contractor software), and 14 operational risks requiring resolution before Phase 2 work can commence.
decisions: Agreement cannot be signed in current state — three critical exhibits empty and work-for-hire claim likely unenforceable
Verbal March 10 layer model (Infrastructure=Licensed, Application=Company IP, Data=MBM property) must be codified as Section 3.6 in binding text
§4.3 ROFR scope is too broad — must be narrowed from entire healthcare admin market to Project-specific competition only
Phase 2 work cannot begin until BAA is executed (Exhibit B placeholder currently violates HIPAA)
commitments: Contractor to populate Exhibit A with complete Pre-Existing IP inventory (MERIDIAN mesh, voice pipeline, frameworks, ATPO tools) — required by §7.1(e) representation before signing
Omar and Contractor to negotiate and fill Exhibit C compensation rates (separate rates for Phase 1 non-HIPAA, Phase 2 HIPAA, architecture consulting) — no timeline specified
Contractor to propose Section 3.6 Layered Architecture Acknowledgment to Omar's attorney for codification into binding text — dependency on Omar's attorney response
Narrow §4.3 ROFR with 30-day exercise deadline and PaaS carve-out — requires negotiation, no deadline given
MBM to execute BAA as Exhibit B before any Phase 2 PHI access — implicit gate, no deadline specified
discoveries: Work-for-hire doctrine doesn't apply to independent contractor custom software — only 9 enumerated categories under 17 U.S.C. § 101, software development not among them
MBM's Act 60 amendment to include SaaS/AI/IP is still pending approval — if denied, entire Puerto Rico tax structure collapses
Ayala PC (NJ entity) payment routing on behalf of MBM (PR entity) is IRS red flag for Act 60 audit scrutiny
Exhibit A Pre-Existing IP inventory is contradicted by known assets: MERIDIAN (17,900+ MIG nodes, 49 sentinels), voice pipeline, deployment tooling all missing from 2-item list
MBM has no technical capacity to independently maintain software — Omar is solo psychiatrist with ~1 week AI research, no engineering team

action_items.what: Populate Exhibit A with comprehensive Pre-Existing IP list (MERIDIAN mesh, voice pipeline, ATPO tools, development frameworks) and obtain Partnership approval for completeness
Negotiate and agree compensation rates for Exhibit C (Phase 1 non-HIPAA website rate, Phase 2 HIPAA clinical platform rate, architecture/consulting rate)
Propose Section 3.6 Layered Architecture Acknowledgment codifying March 10 verbal framework (Infrastructure=Licensed, Application=Company IP, Data=MBM property) into binding text
Narrow §4.3 ROFR from entire healthcare admin domain to Project-specific competition only; add 30-day exercise window with deemed waiver
Add HIPAA gate sentence to §5.5 making executed BAA a hard prerequisite before Phase 2 PHI work begins

calls.preview: Full coherence audit of MBM IP Agreement v3 identified critical gaps (missing BAA, compensation rates, SOW), structural contradictions (work-for-hire doctrine inapplicable to independent contractor software), and 14 operational risks requiring resolution before Phase 2 work can commence.

calls.title: Tactical Assessment — Daemon Tendency Config
calls.file: docs/transcriptions/Tactical Assessment Perfect Daemon Tendency Config 30935e812bbb815aad86e05360104ec7.md
calls.summary: Tactical assessment of daemon tendency configuration
decisions: Adopt four-daemon role framework: Sandman (orchestrator), Delphi (infra architect), Sensei (coherence gate), Atlas (operator surfaces), each with defined primary tendencies, inhibitors, escalation triggers, and bench-down adjustments

Require handoff packages (with standardized schema including owner, task class, goal, success criteria, rollback plan, blast radius, proof artifacts, decision record) as the unit of accountability for all cross-lane work
Establish conflict protocol: Sandman declares decision window, Sensei writes risk memo, Delphi writes feasibility/blast radius, Atlas writes operator impact, then Sandman decides and logs rationale
Enforce authority matrix specifying what each daemon may and must not do (routing/prioritization, infra changes, blocking actions, building surfaces)
Treat bench-down as explicit declared system state with automatic mesh-wide effects (gate tightening, WIP reduction, decision TTLs) when resource constraints are declared
Enable capability envelope expansion during degraded mode with controlled takeover rules when any daemon is unavailable (expansion scope, TTL, budget adjustments)
Delphi has incident-initiator authority to begin recovery actions when SLO is breached but must notify Sandman via handoff package
discoveries: The missing piece for perfect daemon configuration is tendency shaping (decision-making defaults per daemon), not additional capability
Current daemon role boundaries already align with the recommended framework without major restructuring needed
Daemon capability envelopes should expand into adjacent work only during degraded mode, following explicit containment rules

threats.title: EHR Market Saturation + Incumbents
threats.description: Epic, Cerner, Athena Health dominate. 100+ mid-market players. Differentiation on MERIDIAN's distributed mesh could be novel (federated patient records across clinics) but requires deep HIPAA/HL7 expertise.

threats.title: Healthcare Compliance & Liability
threats.description: HIPAA, 21 CFR Part 11 (FDA if any medical device claims), state licensing. Single breach = company-ending liability. Team inexperience in healthcare ops is risk.

threats.title: Scope Creep / Identity Drift
threats.description: MERIDIAN's core identity is distributed AI + memory systems. Healthcare could distract from core IP (mesh orchestration, autonomous agents). Requires clarity on whether this is core product or channel partnership.

threats.title: Levi Dependency
threats.description: If acquisition roll-up is the play, MERIDIAN becomes backend vendor to Levi's operator. Revenue + strategy depend on Levi's execution. No exit optionality if Levi pivots.

tech_requirements: Layered architecture model: Infrastructure Layer (pre-existing tools) + Application Layer (custom business logic) + Data Layer (PHI/clinical data)
Development environment segregation: separate code repositories, project management systems, documentation systems, access credentials
Dedicated infrastructure access credentials for Project work isolated from general business systems
Infrastructure layer tooling with clean licensing boundaries (tools remain Contractor pre-existing IP but licensed to MBM for embedded use only, not standalone product development)
Patent/copyright registration and enforcement infrastructure (per Section 2.5 Further Assurances)
IP classification dispute resolution infrastructure (independent attorney review, binding recommendation model per Section 2.7)
HIPAA-compliant systems for PHI access and handling (Section 5.5, details cut off)
Data segregation controls: patient data, clinical records, practice data remain isolated as MBM exclusive property
integrations: Contractor's proprietary tools/platforms/frameworks (identified in Exhibit A) as licensed dependencies in Infrastructure Layer
Partnership's multi-agentic PaaS API product (mentioned as existing, cross-industry use)
General-purpose tools, platforms, APIs used across multiple industries including healthcare (explicitly carve-out from conflict restrictions)
MBM's proprietary data sets, clinical protocols, practice-specific workflows as inputs to Application Layer
security_compliance: HIPAA compliance (explicit requirement in Section 5.5, full scope cut off)
Protected Health Information (PHI) access controls and handling procedures
Confidentiality framework with layered data access restrictions
Credential and access token segregation between MBM business systems and Project-specific systems
Documented technical specifications for MBM Resources (proprietary software, patient data, clinical protocols, workflows)
build_vs_buy: Infrastructure Layer: USE EXISTING — Contractor's pre-existing tools identified in Exhibit A (pre-existing IP license model)
Application Layer: BUILD CUSTOM — All clinical workflows, practice-specific logic, UIs for MBM specifications are custom builds (Company IP)
Partnership's multi-agentic PaaS API: BUY/USE EXISTING — explicitly carved out as non-competitive (Exhibit A reference)
General-purpose healthcare tools: BUY — allowed for Contractor's other healthcare clients (not restricted if no MBM IP/confidential info used)
Patent/IP enforcement infrastructure: BUILD/BUY — need attorney, filing capability, registration tracking
tech_risks: IP CLASSIFICATION AMBIGUITY — Risk that embedded infrastructure tools get misclassified as Company IP or vice versa. MITIGATION: Section 2.7 independent attorney dispute process + Section 3.6 layered architecture acknowledgment
CROSS-PROJECT CONTAMINATION — Risk that work for other clients contaminates MBM deliverables. MITIGATION: Section 3.5 requires separate code repos, credentials, project management systems

INFRASTRUCTURE LICENSING BOUNDARY EROSION — Risk that licensed tools become tightly coupled to Company IP, making separation impossible. MITIGATION: Section 3.4 requires that embedded tools remain separately identified and that license scope is limited to 'operation, maintenance, and modification of Deliverables, not standalone products'

HIPAA COMPLIANCE DEBT — Section 5.5 incomplete; full scope of PHI handling, audit trails, encryption, BAA requirements unknown. RECOMMEND: Phase 2 gate mentioned; ensure full HIPAA architecture is documented before PHI access begins

ATTORNEY-IN-FACT POWER OVERREACH — Section 2.5 grants MBM power of attorney to execute IP documents on Contractor's behalf. MITIGATION: carve-outs for active disputes, agreements, and requires notice within 5 days

EXHIBIT A MAINTENANCE RISK — Agreement depends heavily on Exhibit A (Contractor tools/platforms/partnership products). If Exhibit A becomes stale, classification disputes multiply. RECOMMEND: formal Exhibit A review cadence (quarterly?)

TOOLING LICENSING DECAY — Contractor pre-existing tools may be vendored/deprecated/acquired by third parties. Licensing model must account for upstream changes. RECOMMEND: clear escalation for upstream licensing changes

decisions: Adopt layered IP architecture (Infrastructure/Application/Data) as governing classification model for all IP disputes
Implement independent IP attorney process for IP classification disputes (30-day resolution, binding unless litigated, equal cost-sharing)
Grant Contractor perpetual, royalty-free, non-exclusive license to tools embedded in Deliverables (but NOT standalone products)
Establish attorney-in-fact power for MBM to execute IP perfection documents if Contractor doesn't respond within 30 days
Require explicit segregation of work (separate repos, credentials, environments) between MBM and Contractor's other operations
opportunities: Layered architecture enables Contractor to build general-purpose PaaS API (Infrastructure Layer) serving multiple industries while MBM owns clinical differentiation (Application Layer)
Right of first refusal creates M&A/partnership pathway if Contractor develops competitive healthcare products (30-day decision window gives time to negotiate)
Independent IP attorney mechanism reduces litigation risk vs. ad-hoc disputes — creates predictable, fair resolution process that both parties trust
Segregation requirements (Section 3.5) enable Contractor to serve non-competing clients in healthcare and other industries without jeopardizing MBM's IP — expands addressable market
Exhibit A (tools list) creates transparency that could lead to future cross-licensing or joint product development
threats: HIPAA compliance section (Article 5) cuts off at 'Phase 2 gate' — unclear what our actual obligations are. If we're handling PHI, incomplete HIPAA terms could expose MBM to regulatory risk.
Right of first refusal is 30-day window — if Contractor/Partnership moves fast on competitive product, we could lose ROFR if we don't respond in time

Infrastructure Layer (Contractor Pre-Existing IP) could be licensed to MBM's healthcare competitors under Section 3.4 if tools are 'embedded in Deliverables' — definition of 'embedded' could create dispute
IP classification disputes still possible despite mechanisms — process adds 45-75 days but doesn't eliminate uncertainty or cost
commitments: MBM commits: Provide isolated development infrastructure and dedicated access credentials for Project work (Section 3.5)
MBM commits: Share 50% of independent IP attorney costs if classification disputes arise (Section 2.7)
MBM commits: Maintain good-faith IP classification process; won't weaponize attorney-in-fact authority during active disputes (Section 2.5(a))
Contractor commits: Segregate all Project work from other business operations; maintain separate repos, environments, documentation (Section 3.5)
Contractor commits: Identify all pre-existing tools in Exhibit A promptly; notify MBM within 30 days of any new competitive product development (Sections 3.4, 4.3)
Both parties commit: Attempt good-faith resolution of IP disputes before escalating to independent attorney (Section 2.7)
CEO.narrative_assessment: POSITIVE — Sophisticated, bilateral framing. Shows we understand Contractor's need for tool reuse across clients while protecting MBM's clinical IP ownership. Layered architecture section is the strongest narrative element: it says 'infrastructure is yours to reuse, but the healthcare application layer is ours.' Right of first refusal is protective without being punitive. Independent attorney mechanism signals we want fair resolution, not litigation. However: HIPAA section is incomplete (cuts at Phase 2 gate) — this MUST be finished before any PHI work or execution. Overall: positions us as thoughtful partner + rigorous IP steward. Recommend completion of Article 5 and Exhibits A/B before signature.
CEO.critical_gap: Article 5 (HIPAA Compliance) is incomplete — document cuts off at 'Phase 2 gate'. If Contractor will access patient data, this is non-negotiable. Unclear what our compliance obligations are, what Business Associate Agreement language is required, or whether Phase 2 gating means we're NOT handling PHI initially. MUST complete and review with healthcare legal counsel before any execution.

However: (1) Didn't claim MERIDIAN as the platform others should use — positioned as 'here's what we built' not 'here's what you should buy'; (2) Group independently converging on same pattern = validation + threat; (3) Missed opportunity to position MERIDIAN as the 'OpenClaw for enterprises' or 'autonomous agent framework.' Recommend: sharpen narrative to claim MERIDIAN as the *platform* for heartbeat-driven agents, not just internal infrastructure. Otherwise, this becomes a case study others learn from and replicate.

CEO.narrative_assessment: WEAK on reliability, STRONG on innovation. You're showing impressive autonomous capabilities (self-healing, no-human-interaction runs), but the conversation is dominated by operational pain (interruptions, lost context, lack of graceful shutdown). When compared to external systems, you lose on functional completeness and speed-to-hand-off. **Fix needed**: Position Meridian as 'reliable autonomous system' not 'experimental playground.' The A-B system and task resumption logging must be shipped before the next public-facing demo. The NotebookLM integration is good strategy (leverage external RAG vs. build) — lean into this in narratives about efficiency. The YouTube→audit→GitHub→vote→build pipeline is your strongest signal of product-market fit; showcase this more.

process_gaps.evidence: 'Sometimes what happens is we have a functional system, and it's working, and then we add a bunch of stuff. And then the next day is less functional.'

process_gaps.evidence: Daemon asked 'how can we make $50?' responded with lecture on why that's a waste. Senior had to manually correct: 'log this so you learn from it.'

process_gaps.gap: Memory and storage issues only discovered through active human sessions

process_gaps.evidence: 'Another thing the bots have been running into issues of memory and storage.' Storage bloat filled VPS multiple times before discovered.

process_gaps.gap: Feature output doesn't match specification

process_gaps.evidence: 'I wanted [cheat sheet] on all of the pages of the overlays with the little info button...but it did create like a long ass cheat sheet.' Correct output, wrong format.

process_gaps.gap: No systematic knowledge transfer from manual corrections to daemon behavior

threats: Storage bloat: unmonitored process created multi-GB files over short period, nearly filled VPS storage. Indicates lack of resource guardrails on daemon operations

Daemon mistake logging and use as training signal for next iteration (ongoing)
Test 'converge' protocol on additional strategic initiatives beyond revenue planning
CEO.narrative_assessment: STRONG. The narrative demonstrates technical maturity and realistic expectations: acknowledging system limits ('bots are echoes of decisions we made'), being rigorous about measurement ('what if we change this weight from 6 to 7?'), and learning from operational experience ('we're the dumb dumb, not the system'). The CEO is pragmatic (manual unblocking while infrastructure improves), self-aware (honest about infrastructure gaps), and appreciative of autonomous capability when properly aligned (impressed by converge revenue planning outputs). Slight risk: internal friction over daemon compliance ('you just did the opposite of what I said') — suggests either instruction clarity gap or daemon value misalignment that needs addressing. Overall positioning: 'We're building sophisticated multi-agent infrastructure with clear-eyed understanding of current limits and disciplined approach to improvement.' This is credible for investors and partners.

CEO.narrative_assessment: This research agenda reveals sophisticated DoD/government contracting awareness, but **lack of a unified narrative thread**. CEO should clarify: Is MERIDIAN a government contractor? A consulting firm? A staffing platform? An infrastructure vendor? The breadth (11 DoD/revenue vectors, 3 platform/hiring vectors, 1 tax strategy) suggests opportunistic exploration rather than coherent positioning. **Strengths:** Production multi-agent system + audit metrics are genuine differentiators in GraphRAG/agentic AI niches. NATS expertise is rare. **Gaps:** No mention of customer discovery or founder-customer fit validation for any of these vectors. No stated competitive positioning or defensibility claim. Recommend: (1) Narrow to 2-3 primary go-to-markets, (2) Validate one customer/contract before expanding, (3) Build a coherent positioning story that ties mesh infrastructure expertise → fractional AI CTO → DoD contracting together, (4) Address the uncomfortable question: 'Are we a 3-person team with an impressive system, or are we building a venture-scale business?'

decisions.decision: Build local demo video pipeline on M1 to replace cloud Cursor agent ($40-100/run)
decisions.rationale: Replicate ~80% of Cursor's auto-demo capability using existing tools (Playwright, ffmpeg) at zero marginal cost
decisions.impact: Eliminates per-demo generation costs; unblocks frequent demo iteration in feature development

decisions.decision: Centralize cursor agent task queue with CLI management (cursor_claim.py)
decisions.rationale: Prior queue was write-only — stale entries accumulated unresolved. Queue now has claim/done/abandon/list subcommands with parallel session protocol
decisions.impact: Enables coordinated multi-session cursor agent work without file conflicts; enables throughput scaling

decisions.decision: Move daemon restart strategy from flat 120s cooldown to exponential backoff (#321)
decisions.rationale: Reduces cascade failures when a daemon repeatedly crashes; configurable via health.yaml

decisions.decision: Implement tiered alert routing by severity (#324)
decisions.rationale: CRITICAL → direct Telegram DM to Alex; WARNING → channel bridge; INFO → event stream only. Reduces noise, ensures critical issues surface immediately
decisions.impact: Improves on-call experience; prevents alert fatigue while maintaining visibility

opportunities.opportunity: Demo video cost elimination unlocks product marketing velocity
opportunities.details: Moving from $40-100/run to zero marginal cost means we can generate polished demos for every feature PR. Previously cost-prohibitive. Enables: rapid customer onboarding videos, product marketing, feature validation

opportunities.opportunity: SRE metrics framework now quantifies system health (Metric #20a-d)
opportunities.details: #323 adds Cypher-backed queries for MTTR, recurrence rate, false positive rate, remediation success rate with targets (e.g., >85% remediation success). Improves observability of reliability posture

opportunities.opportunity: Cursor agent parallel execution capacity increases with queue governance
opportunities.details: Max 2 parallel sessions with non-overlapping file scopes is now enforced. Enables higher feature velocity by coordinating independent work streams

threats.threat: Queue data quality degradation (stale entries, duplicate #474 assignments)
threats.details: Two old #474 entries exist (`1f7f8cea`, `22be6d87`); one #913 entry has malformed path. Risk: conflicting work assignments, wasted effort on outdated specs. Immediate fix: mark stale #474 entries done before starting new #351/#474 session
threats.mitigation: Implement queue janitor task (monthly cleanup) + audit queue before major dispatches

threats.threat: Demo pipeline robustness gaps (UUID filename detection, libfreetype)
threats.details: libfreetype missing in Homebrew ffmpeg → title overlay silently falls back to scaled video (acceptable). Playwright UUID filename detection via before/after snapshot — if snapshot fails, video encoding is skipped silently with no error. Risk: broken demos without notice to user
threats.mitigation: Add explicit error logging if snapshot diff fails; consider adding validation step to pipeline

threats.threat: Narrative misalignment: demo videos not framed in product/market strategy
threats.details: Transcript focuses on technical implementation but doesn't articulate *why* demo videos matter (customer onboarding? marketing? validation?). If this is critical to go-to-market, the framing should be clear to the team
threats.mitigation: Document demo video use cases in product strategy; tie to customer acquisition or retention metric

strengths: Technical rigor: 3-round plan audit caught real gaps (dead Remotion refs, broken GitHub strategy, ffmpeg artifacts)
Cost optimization: $0-cost demo pipeline vs. $40-100/run cloud is a strong competitive signal
Operational maturity: Formalized queue with CLI, parallel session protocol, smoke test discipline
Resilience mindset: Graceful fallbacks (libfreetype missing) rather than hard failures
weaknesses: Missing 'why': Transcript doesn't articulate why demo videos are strategically important (customer acquisition? marketing? technical validation?)
Queue immaturity: Stale entries and malformed data suggest governance is new; reliability not yet proven
Narrative gap: Team doesn't collectively frame the demo pipeline in product/market terms — it's presented as cost savings only
narrative_assessment.overall: Internally strong execution and technical judgment. Externally, the narrative needs tightening: frame demos as a **product velocity multiplier** (enable rapid customer onboarding, marketing, feature validation) not just as a cost saving. This reframes the work from operational efficiency to customer-facing capability.

items: Handoff Package v1 format + audit infrastructure
delivery_timelines.reason: No dates, milestones, or sequencing specified. Proposes significant coordination infrastructure with no phased rollout plan.

resource_needs.purpose: Track conflict resolutions, tendencies, escalations, bench-down state transitions
Enforce decision windows and evidence checkpoints

dependencies.description: Handoff packages must be logged and trackable for auditing and reconciliation
Requires risk memo from Sensei, feasibility from Delphi, UX/ops impact from Atlas, decision from Sandman
Sensei blocks unauthorized mutations, Delphi implements allowed changes
Sandman declares state, all daemons tighten WIP/TTL/TTE values
Requires real-time knowledge of which daemon is down to trigger capability envelope expansion
Summary must become reference material for ops procedures

tech_requirements: Handoff package format enforcement (v1: owner, task class, goal, success criteria, rollback plan, blast radius, next evidence, proof artifacts, decision record)
Escalation trigger system with TTL (time-to-live) enforcement and decision window declarations
Decision record audit trail with 'what/who/when' and evidence checkpoints
Authority matrix enforcement layer (role-based access control for daemon mutations)
Bench-down mode state machine with explicit entry/exit criteria and automatic global effects
Degraded mode capability envelope expansion/contraction based on daemon availability
Evidence collection and validation checkpoint system (TTE: time-to-evidence thresholds)
Conflict resolution protocol implementation (5-step deadlock resolution for incompatible plans)
WIP (work-in-progress) limits and budget tracking per daemon per mode
Daemon state visibility system (which daemon down, scope of expansion, TTL for degraded mode)
integrations: NATS messaging (daemon-to-daemon handoff packages and escalation signals)
Neo4j MIG (graph schema mutation gating, coherence decisions)
Dashboard/monitoring for daemon status and capability envelope state
Audit trail backend (PostgreSQL/Neo4j for decision records and conflict logs)
Alert/scheduler system for TTL-based escalation triggers (Pulse system or equivalent)
Operator surfaces (Atlas-owned) for degraded mode declarations and conflict resolution UI
security_compliance: Identity/auth policy changes require Sensei sign-off + audit artifacts before Delphi implementation
Graph schema mutations gated by Sensei (no direct execution allowed)
Infra config changes limited to Delphi within change windows with rollback constraints
Security policy mutations require documented justification and next-evidence requirements
Reversibility bias: all actions must be idempotent or explicitly rollback-capable
Blast radius tracking mandatory for every cross-daemon handoff
Change control windows (Delphi entry restricted during bench-down or incident states)
build_vs_buy: BUILD: Custom daemon coordination/tendency configuration layer (MERIDIAN-specific, no COTS product fits lane-based governance)
BUILD: Conflict resolution protocol automation (5-step decision gate with risk memo requirements)
BUILD: Authority matrix enforcer (role-based action validation tied to daemon identity)
BUILD: Degraded mode state machine (custom orchestration for daemon unavailability scenarios)
BUILD: Handoff package validator and audit trail system (integrated with existing Neo4j/decision logging)
tech_risks: Single points of failure: Sandman (routing), Delphi (execution), Sensei (coherence gate) — degraded mode takeover rules may cause service degradation or conflicting decisions
TTL-based escalation triggers may create race conditions if evidence arrives between decision window and timeout
Handoff package format enforcement not currently automated — manual compliance risk
Conflict resolution protocol can deadlock if Sandman decision window expires before evidence collected
Degraded mode 'capability envelope expansion' increases blast radius risk — adjacent-lane work by unprepared daemons may introduce bugs
Decision logging overhead may slow high-velocity work (evidence collection before action)
Evidence checkpoint SLOs not yet defined — 'TTE' targets needed to prevent indefinite blocking
Takeover rules for daemon-down scenarios incomplete (spec cuts off mid-sentence for 'If Delphi is down')
No explicit mechanism to prevent competing daemon actions during conflict resolution window
Bench-down mode entry criteria defined but exit criteria vague ('SLO stable for a defined window' — window unspecified)

decisions: Formalize daemon lane separation: each daemon has a primary tendency (Sandman=routing, Delphi=reliability, Sensei=coherence gating, Atlas=operator surfaces) with explicit counter-tendencies that inhibit cross-lane drift
Adopt evidence-first escalation: no escalation or decision made without measurable signals, proofs, or bounded decision TTLs
Implement Handoff Package v1 as mandatory for cross-lane work (owner, task class, success criteria, rollback plan, blast radius, proof artifacts, decision record)
Establish Bench-Down as an explicit, declared system state with entry/exit criteria and automatic effects (gate strictness increase, surface freeze, recovery-only posture)

Create Conflict Protocol: when daemons propose incompatible plans, Sandman declares window, Sensei writes risk memo, Delphi writes feasibility, Atlas writes operator impact, then Sandman decides (not consensus)
opportunities: Reduce operational complexity and team cognitive load by formalizing roles instead of relying on implicit understanding
Enable safe degraded-mode operation (one daemon down) via explicit takeover rules and capability envelope expansion, increasing mesh resilience
Build a reusable conflict resolution and decision-logging discipline that scales as the team grows or takes on more daemons
Create an auditable decision record (handoff packages + decision logs) that becomes institutional knowledge and reduces re-litigating the same arguments
Shift from reactive incident response to proactive evidence collection (TTE budgets, WIP limits) that prevents escalations in the first place
threats: Lane rigidity could create bottlenecks: strict separation may slow decisions when collaboration would be faster (e.g., Sandman waiting for Delphi feasibility study delays routing)
Overspecification risk: too many protocols (handoff package, conflict resolution, authority matrix, bench-down) could overwhelm operators and become compliance theater instead of safety practice
Hidden single points of failure: if Sandman is the sole router, Sandman unavailability creates cascading delays even with explicit takeover rules; degraded-mode testing must be taken seriously
Escalation timer creep: decision TTLs and evidence windows may be set too tight by operators under pressure, leading to insufficient evidence and regrettable reversals
commitments: Implement and document Handoff Package v1 format (with required fields: owner, task class, success criteria, rollback plan, blast radius, proofs, decision record) across all cross-lane work
Publish Authority Matrix (who can mutate what) and enforce via checklists or automation (e.g., gating CI/CD on Sensei sign-off for graph schema changes)
Declare entry/exit criteria for Bench-Down and create a state machine or dashboard that makes it visible to all daemons (critical for coordinating behavior changes)
Document takeover rules for degraded modes (daemon unavailable) and schedule quarterly drills to validate they work in practice
Define TTE (time-to-evidence) and TTL (decision time-to-live) budgets per task class and publish them in operator runbooks
CEO.narrative_assessment: Strong internal-clarity document that demonstrates mature thinking about autonomy, safety, and operational discipline. Frames daemons as role-bound agents with clear inhibitions and escalation authorities—coherent with modern DevOps/SRE philosophy. However, the narrative *externally* may not yet translate well: the document uses technical jargon (handoff packages, bench-down, capability envelopes) that external stakeholders or board members may struggle to map to business value. Recommend a 2-layer narrative: (1) internal technical spec (this), and (2) external narrative emphasizing *what this enables* (faster incident response, fewer stuck decisions, safe partial-outage operation). The 60-second exec summary is good but incomplete—missing 'why this reduces risk' and 'what happens if we don't do this.' Vision alignment is strong *within the technical team* but needs translation before it can inform board-level decisions about system investment or resilience targets.
